planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Annabelle performs well in morning sun with afternoon shade in zone 9 — full sun in the Gulf South causes wilting and bleaching, while deep shade reduces flower production.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Annabelle is the most famous and most widely planted smooth hydrangea cultivar — a native American species selected for its exceptionally large, pure-white, snowball-like flower heads that can reach 12 inches or more in diameter and weigh enough to bend the stems dramatically in rain. Annabelle was selected from a specimen found growing wild near Anna, Illinois, and has been in continuous cultivation since the 1960s. The extraordinarily large, spherical flower heads in brilliant white are one of the great spectacles of the summer garden in shade — there is almost nothing else that produces flower heads of this size in partial to full shade. Annabelle blooms on new wood, meaning it can be cut back hard each spring (to 12 inches) and will still flower the same season — unlike old-wood bloomers that lose flowers if cut incorrectly. This makes it nearly foolproof: any pruning approach that removes old stems in late winter produces abundant bloom. In zone 9, Annabelle blooms from May through July and handles summer heat in partial shade better than Hydrangea macrophylla. The flowers dry beautifully on the plant, transitioning from fresh white to paper-white to soft tan as they age through summer into fall. Divide or transplant in fall after dormancy.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Limelight tolerates full sun in zone 9 better than French hydrangea but benefits from afternoon shade — morning sun with afternoon shade maintains the attractive chartreuse color longer before it fades to white.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Limelight is the most celebrated panicle hydrangea cultivar — a Dutch introduction that has become one of the most popular landscape shrubs in America in the early twenty-first century, distinguished from the classic PeeGee hydrangea (Hydrangea paniculata) by its unique flower color: the large, cone-shaped flower panicles (10–12 inches long) open in a distinctive pale chartreuse-green rather than white, gradually changing to creamy white as they mature, then transitioning to dusty pink and finally parchment-buff as they age through summer into fall — providing nearly five months of evolving ornamental interest from a single set of flowers. The opening chartreuse color is particularly distinctive and unusual in the landscape — fresh, cool-looking, and completely unlike any other flowering shrub color. Limelight blooms on new wood and can be pruned hard in late winter to control size and encourage vigorous new shoots with maximum flower production. In zone 9, Limelight performs well in morning sun and afternoon shade; full day sun may cause wilting during peak summer heat. The large flower heads dry beautifully on the plant for fall and winter ornamental interest.

common_name: Nikko Blue Hydrangea (Classic Blue Mophead Hydrangea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5 for blue flowers), well-drained soil in partial shade; Nikko Blue flowers blue in acidic soil (pH below 5.5) and pink in alkaline soil (pH above 6.5) — add aluminum sulfate to lower pH and intensify the blue color in Gulf South alkaline soils.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Nikko Blue is the most widely grown blue mophead hydrangea cultivar and a classic of the American garden — producing large (8–10 inch), rounded, fully double, sky-blue to cornflower-blue flower heads in early summer from June through August. The iconic blue mophead hydrangea flower is inseparable from the image of the classic American coastal and southern garden, and Nikko Blue has been the most popular blue mophead for decades. The characteristic pH-dependent color change is part of Nikko Blue's management: acidic soil (pH 5.0–5.5) produces the richest true blue; alkaline soil (pH above 6.5) produces pink. In Gulf South alkaline soils, amending with aluminum sulfate (2 lbs per 100 sq ft) and acidifying fertilizers in spring maintains the blue color. Nikko Blue blooms on old wood — meaning the flower buds form in late summer for the following year's bloom. This means the primary rule is: do NOT prune in fall, winter, or spring before bloom; prune only by removing spent flowers immediately after bloom to shape. In zone 9, Nikko Blue is evergreen to semi-evergreen and performs best in morning sun with afternoon protection from the intense Gulf South summer heat.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Incrediball requires partial shade in zone 9 — full afternoon sun causes significant wilting and bleaching of the enormous flower heads.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Incrediball is the improved Annabelle — a next-generation smooth hydrangea cultivar bred with significantly stronger, stiffer stems than the classic Annabelle that was notorious for stem flopping under the weight of the enormous flower heads in rain. The problem with Annabelle was that the giant 10–12 inch white flower heads would weigh down and collapse the flexible stems in wet weather, leaving the entire plant prostrate on the ground. Incrediball solves this with substantially stronger stems that hold the enormous flower heads upright even in heavy rain and wet conditions. The flower heads are similarly giant — up to 12 inches in diameter — in the same brilliant white as Annabelle, and the plant has the same new-wood blooming habit (can be cut back hard in late winter and still flowers prolifically the same season). In every practical characteristic, Incrediball is an upgrade over Annabelle: same giant white flowers, same performance in shade, same easy care, but with stems strong enough to support the flowers in all conditions. Like Annabelle, Incrediball is one of the most cold-hardy hydrangeas (to zone 3) and one of the most reliable bloomers across a wide range of conditions in zone 9 with afternoon shade.

common_name: Pinky Winky Hydrangea (Two-Tone Pink-and-White Panicle Hydrangea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Pinky Winky performs best with full morning sun and afternoon shade in zone 9 — sufficient sun for maximum flower production with shade protection during peak summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Pinky Winky is a distinctive panicle hydrangea cultivar distinguished by its unusual two-tone flower display — the large, conical (12–16 inch) flower panicles open pure white in midsummer and then progressively change color from the bottom of the panicle upward, with the lower, older florets turning from white to deep pink to rose-red while the upper, younger florets remain white. This creates a striking bicolor effect of white-on-pink within a single flower head that provides weeks of ornamental interest as the color change progresses. The vigorous, upright-arching habit grows more vigorously than Limelight — Pinky Winky can reach 6–8 feet tall with strong, upright stems. Like all Hydrangea paniculata cultivars, Pinky Winky blooms on new wood and can be pruned hard in late winter to stimulate maximum new growth and flower production. It is among the hardiest of all hydrangeas (to zone 3) and tolerates clay soils, full sun (with adequate moisture), and some drought better than French hydrangeas. In zone 9, the pink color develops most intensely in cooler fall temperatures and may be less vivid in very hot summers.
